variables.U: Potential energy as a function of position (J)

locked_facts: Force from potential energy: F = -dU/dx (negative gradient of U)
Equilibrium where F = 0, i.e., dU/dx = 0 (slope of U-x curve is zero)
Stable equilibrium at U minimum (d²U/dx² > 0): restoring force
Unstable equilibrium at U maximum (d²U/dx² < 0): displacing force
Turning points where KE = 0, i.e., E = U(x): particle reverses direction
Particle is confined to regions where E ≥ U(x) — cannot enter classically forbidden region where U > E
truth_statements: The force on a particle can be derived from the potential energy curve as the negative of its slope
At a minimum of the U-x curve, the equilibrium is stable — small displacement produces restoring force
At a maximum of the U-x curve, the equilibrium is unstable — small displacement drives particle away
The kinetic energy at any position equals total energy minus potential energy: KE = E - U(x)
Turning points are positions where the total energy line intersects the U(x) curve — particle momentarily stops and reverses

aha_moment.what_to_show: A ball rolling on a U(x) landscape. At a U minimum, the ball oscillates — push it slightly left or right and it rolls back (stable). At a U maximum, the ball perches precariously — the slightest push sends it rolling away (unstable). The force arrow always points downhill on the U curve.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Force arrows derived from -dU/dx slope. At minimum: arrows point inward (restoring). At maximum: arrows point outward (displacing). Ball motion matches force direction.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that force direction comes from the SLOPE of U, not the value of U — high U does not mean strong force

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: A U(x) curve displayed with wells and hills. Total energy E shown as a horizontal dashed line. Particle placed at a position where E > U(x). KE = E - U(x) shown as vertical gap.
simulation_states.emphasis: The gap between E line and U curve IS the kinetic energy at each point

simulation_states.label: Buildup
simulation_states.description: Particle moves along the U curve. At steep slopes: large force, fast acceleration. At flat regions: near-zero force, constant velocity. Force arrow F = -dU/dx drawn at particle position, always pointing downhill.
simulation_states.emphasis: Force equals negative slope — steeper U curve = larger force on particle

simulation_states.label: Aha
simulation_states.description: Particle reaches a turning point where E = U(x): KE = 0, particle stops and reverses. Then oscillates in a potential well (stable equilibrium). Placed at a U maximum: slightest perturbation sends it rolling down (unstable).
simulation_states.emphasis: Turning point: E line meets U curve. Stable minimum: restoring force. Unstable maximum: displacing force.

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive: drag the total energy line E up or down to change accessible region. More energy = wider oscillation, can escape wells. Adjust U curve shape. Click any point to see force and KE values.
simulation_states.emphasis: Raise E to escape the potential well. Lower E to confine particle. Force always from slope.

common_misconceptions.belief: Higher potential energy means stronger force on the particle
trigger_phrases: potential energy zyada toh force bhi zyada
common_misconceptions.correction: Force depends on the SLOPE of U(x), not its value. F = -dU/dx. A flat region at high U has zero force. A steep region at low U has large force. It's the gradient that matters.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show flat plateau at high U with F = 0, and steep slope at low U with large F arrow

common_misconceptions.belief: A particle can exist in regions where potential energy exceeds total energy
trigger_phrases: U > E mein ja sakta hai
common_misconceptions.correction: Classically, KE = E - U(x) must be ≥ 0. If U(x) > E, then KE would be negative (impossible). The particle is confined to regions where E ≥ U(x). It reverses at turning points where E = U(x).
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Shade forbidden region where U > E in red. Particle bounces back at turning points.

legend.meaning: Potential energy as function of position
